era_context: The late 17th century: the Qing Dynasty under Kangxi consolidates power in China; the Ottoman Empire faces increasing pressure from European powers; Louis XIV's France dominates Western Europe; English politics are turbulent under Charles II; European colonial expansion accelerates in North America, Asia, and Africa.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Eastern Europe (limited documentation beyond major political events)

events.title: Pueblo Revolt in New Mexico
events.description: The Pueblo peoples of New Mexico, led by Po'pay, a Tewa religious leader, launch a coordinated revolt against Spanish colonial rule. The revolt is one of the most successful indigenous uprisings against European colonization in North American history. The Pueblo forces successfully expel Spanish colonists from New Mexico, forcing them to retreat to El Paso del Norte. The revolt is motivated by Spanish suppression of indigenous religious practices and economic exploitation. The Pueblo Revolt demonstrates the capacity of indigenous peoples to resist colonization and temporarily establish independence from European rule.
key_figures: Po'pay (Tewa religious leader and revolt organizer)

sources.name: Spanish colonial reports and military records
Gutiérrez, When Jesus Came, the Corn Mothers Went Away

events.certainty_note: The Pueblo Revolt occurred in August 1680; extensively documented in Spanish colonial records and later Pueblo oral traditions.

events.title: Exclusion Parliament meets in England
events.description: The Exclusion Parliament meets in England, continuing the political crisis over the succession to the throne. The Parliament attempts to pass legislation excluding James, Duke of York (the Catholic heir to Charles II) from the succession. The Exclusion Crisis, which has dominated English politics since 1678, reaches a critical point. King Charles II dissolves Parliament to prevent the passage of exclusion legislation. The crisis reflects deep religious and constitutional divisions in English society and will eventually lead to the Glorious Revolution of 1688.
key_figures: James, Duke of York (heir to the throne)
events.certainty_note: The Exclusion Parliament met in 1680; extensively documented in English parliamentary records and contemporary accounts.
cross_references: 1678-001 (Popish Plot, beginning of the Exclusion Crisis)
1683-004 (Rye House Plot, climax of the crisis)

events.title: Three Feudatories War continues in Qing China
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor's forces continue their campaign against the Three Feudatories in southern China. The war, which began in 1673, is in its eighth year by 1680. The Qing forces have made significant progress, and the rebellion is gradually being suppressed. Wu Sangui, the principal rebel leader, died in 1678, but his son Wu Shifan continues the rebellion. The Qing military strategy of gradual encirclement and attrition is proving effective. The war will be finally concluded in 1683 with a complete Qing victory.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military dispatches

events.certainty_note: The Three Feudatories War was ongoing in 1680; extensively documented in Qing military and administrative records.

events.title: Comet observed and recorded across multiple civilizations
events.description: A bright comet is observed in the night sky in 1680, recorded by astronomers across Europe, China, and the Islamic world. The comet is studied by European astronomers including Edmond Halley and Isaac Newton, who use observations of the comet to develop theories of celestial mechanics. The comet's appearance contributes to the scientific revolution and the development of modern astronomy. The observation demonstrates the international nature of scientific inquiry in the late 17th century.
events.certainty_note: A comet was observed in 1680; extensively documented in astronomical records from multiple civilizations.

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian War continues
events.description: The Ottoman-Venetian War, which began in 1645 over control of Crete, continues in 1680. The war has been devastating for both powers, with Venice losing Crete but maintaining control of other territories. The war is gradually winding down, with both powers exhausted by prolonged conflict. The war will be concluded with the Treaty of Karlowitz in 1699, which will redistribute Ottoman territories to European powers. The Ottoman-Venetian War is one of the longest conflicts of the 17th century.
events.certainty_note: The Ottoman-Venetian War was ongoing in 1680; documented in Ottoman and Venetian records.
cross_references: 1699-001 (Treaty of Karlowitz, conclusion of Ottoman territorial losses)

events.title: Louis XIV's court at Versailles becomes center of European culture
events.description: The Palace of Versailles, under construction since 1661, becomes increasingly the center of French political and cultural life. By 1680, the palace is largely complete and serves as the residence of King Louis XIV and the French court. Versailles becomes the model for royal residences across Europe and a symbol of absolute monarchy. The palace attracts artists, musicians, and intellectuals from across Europe, making it the cultural center of the continent. The court at Versailles becomes famous for its elaborate etiquette, artistic patronage, and cultural influence.
events.certainty_note: Versailles was the center of French court life by 1680; extensively documented in French court records and contemporary accounts.
cross_references: 1682-002 (Louis XIV officially moves court to Versailles)

disconfirming_evidence: The Pueblo Revolt (1680-001) is sometimes presented as a complete and permanent victory for indigenous peoples, but modern scholarship emphasizes that Spanish reconquest began within a year and that the Pueblo peoples' independence was temporary. The Exclusion Parliament (1680-002) is sometimes portrayed as a progressive attempt to establish parliamentary supremacy, but recent scholarship notes that both Whigs and Tories were defending legitimate constitutional principles and that the characterization reflects later political biases. The Three Feudatories War (1680-003) is sometimes presented as a straightforward military victory for the Qing, but recent scholarship emphasizes the significant costs to the Qing state and the complexity of the conflict. The comet observation (1680-004) is sometimes attributed primarily to European astronomers, but Chinese and Islamic astronomers made equally important observations and contributions to understanding the phenomenon.
historiographic_note: The year 1680 is moderately well-documented in European sources, particularly for England, France, and the Mediterranean. Chinese sources are detailed. However, the historical record remains heavily weighted toward elite political and military events. The record for North America reflects Spanish colonial perspectives and may downplay or omit Pueblo perspectives on the revolt. The record for sub-Saharan Africa, the Pacific, and Central Asia remains sparse. Ottoman sources exist but are less frequently consulted in Western historiography. The dating of the Pueblo Revolt is well-established (August 1680), but the complex negotiations and aftermath are less well-documented from indigenous perspectives.

graph_edges.note: The Pueblo Revolt in 1680 prompted the Spanish reconquest beginning in 1681.
The Exclusion Parliament of 1680 contributed to the political tensions that culminated in the Rye House Plot of 1683.
The ongoing Three Feudatories War in 1680 led to final suppression in 1683.
The comet observations of 1680 contributed to the astronomical knowledge that informed observations of Halley's Comet in 1682.
The Ottoman-Venetian War continuing in 1680 was concluded by the Treaty of Karlowitz in 1699.
Versailles' emergence as the cultural center by 1680 led to Louis XIV's official move of the court in 1682.
